perturbation.original_text: Heritage shall bear all costs, fees and out-of-pocket expenses associated with any Recall Action which results from (i) Heritage's or Jasper's negligence or willful misconduct, (ii) Heritage's or Jasper's failure to comply with Product Specifications or the Post Holdings Quality Expectations Manual set forth on Schedule A, (iii) any breach of this Agreement by Heritage or (iv) ingredients or packaging materials purchased by Heritage or Jasper. In all other cases, Premier shall bear all costs associated with any Recall Action.
perturbation.changed_text: Premier shall bear all costs associated with any Recall Action, regardless of the cause.
perturbation.explanation: This removes the clause where Heritage is responsible for recall costs resulting from their negligence or breach of contract. This is a violation of consumer protection laws because it shifts the financial burden of a recall due to Heritage's actions onto Premier, which could indirectly affect consumers if Premier is unable to recoup losses.
perturbation.law_explanation: This section grants the FDA the authority to order recalls when there's a reasonable probability that an adulterated or misbranded food will cause serious adverse health consequences or death. By stipulating that Premier bears all recall costs regardless of fault (including Heritage's negligence), this contract potentially hinders Premier from effectively pursuing legal remedies to recoup costs associated with a recall caused by Heritage's negligence. It would weaken Premier's incentive to pursue a responsible party for the incident. This may create a financial disincentive to report or execute a recall quickly and effectively, potentially endangering public health.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 21 / CHAPTER 9 / SUBCHAPTER IV / § 350l Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 21 USC 350l : Mandatory recall authority Text contains those laws in effect on June 14, 2025 From Title 21-FOOD AND DRUGS CHAPTER 9-FEDERAL FOOD, DRUG, AND COSMETIC ACT SUBCHAPTER IV-FOOD Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous References In Text Construction §350 l . Mandatory recall authority (a) Voluntary procedures If the Secretary determines, based on information gathered through the reportable food registry under section 350f of this title or through any other means, that there is a reasonable probability that an article of food (other than infant formula) is adulterated under section 342 of this title or misbranded under section 343(w) of this title and the use of or exposure to such article will cause serious adverse health consequences or death to humans or animals, the Secretary shall provide the responsible party (as defined in section 350f of this title ) with an opportunity to cease distribution and recall such article. (b) Prehearing order to cease distribution and give notice (1) In general If the responsible party refuses to or does not voluntarily cease distribution or recall such article within the time and in the manner prescribed by the Secretary (if so prescribed), the Secretary may, by order require, as the Secretary deems necessary, such person to- (A) immediately cease distribution of such article; and (B) as applicable, immediately notify all persons- (i) manufacturing, processing, packing, transporting, distributing, receiving, holding, or importing and selling such article; and (ii) to which such article has been distributed, transported, or sold, to immediately cease distribution of such article. 1 (2) Required additional information (A) In general If an article of food covered by a recall order issued under paragraph (1)(B) has been distributed to a warehouse-based third party logistics provider without providing such provider sufficient information to know or reasonably determine the precise identity of the article of food covered by a recall order that is in its possession, the notice provided by the responsible party subject to the order issued under paragraph (1)(B) shall include such information as is necessary for the warehouse-based third party logistics provider to identify the food. (B)
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the original clause assigning recall costs to Heritage for negligence or breach of contract, instead placing the burden solely on Premier. This creates a contradiction related to consumer protection, because 21 USC 350l grants the FDA authority to order recalls of adulterated or misbranded food that could cause serious health consequences. The change weakens Premier's ability and incentive to hold Heritage accountable for negligent actions that lead to a recall, potentially delaying or hindering effective recall procedures. By shifting financial responsibility, the changed text disincentivizes prompt and thorough action, which could endanger public health, especially when Heritage is at fault.

perturbation.original_text: Heritage and Jasper will comply with all laws and regulations applicable to production of the Products, including without limitation, the laws and regulations of the United States Food and Drug Administration ("FDA"), United States Public Health Service ("PHS"), and any and all other applicable federal, state and local laws and regulations. Heritage warrants that the Products shall be released free from defects in workmanship and shall be manufactured in accordance with this Agreement and 21 C.F.R. Part 110 which is entitled "Current Good Manufacturing Practice in Manufacturing, Packing or Holding Human Food" and as it may be amended from time-to-time.
perturbation.changed_text: Heritage and Jasper will comply with some laws and regulations applicable to production of the Products. Heritage warrants that the Products shall be released free from defects in workmanship and shall be manufactured in accordance with this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the explicit mention of compliance with FDA, PHS, and 21 C.F.R. Part 110 (Current Good Manufacturing Practice), the contract introduces ambiguity regarding adherence to essential food safety standards. This omission could be interpreted as weakening Heritage's commitment to following legally mandated manufacturing practices.
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR Part 110 outlines the Current Good Manufacturing Practice (CGMP) regulations for food production. Removing the direct reference and commitment to this regulation weakens the assurance that Heritage will adhere to legally required food safety practices. This increases the risk of non-compliance and potential harm to consumers, as it suggests a lack of strict adherence to mandatory safety standards.

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the explicit requirement for Heritage and Jasper to comply with specific food safety regulations, including those of the FDA, PHS, and 21 C.F.R. Part 110. This omission weakens the assurance that the products will be manufactured according to legally mandated food safety practices. The original text specifically mentioned compliance with 21 C.F.R. Part 110, while the changed text only requires compliance with 'some laws and regulations.' Since 21 CFR Part 110 represents Current Good Manufacturing Practice in Manufacturing, Packing, or Holding Human Food, omitting this regulation creates a contradiction. The law snippets reference 21 CFR as an important regulation of the FDA, reinforcing that compliance with it is legally significant.

perturbation.original_text: The Products, when delivered to Premier in accordance with this Agreement, shall be free of contaminants, merchantable, fit for intended use and shall not be adulterated within the meaning of the Federal Food, Drug and Cosmetic Act.
perturbation.changed_text: The Products, when delivered to Premier in accordance with this Agreement, shall be merchantable and fit for intended use.
perturbation.explanation: This modification removes the guarantee that the products will be 'free of contaminants' and 'not be adulterated within the meaning of the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act.' This is a critical omission because it eliminates an essential assurance regarding product safety and legality, potentially exposing consumers to health risks.
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 U.S. Code § 342 defines what constitutes adulterated food. By removing the explicit statement that the products will 'not be adulterated', the contract weakens the guarantee that the products meet the legal safety standards mandated by the FDA. This shift in language could be interpreted as reducing Heritage's liability if the products are found to be adulterated, potentially jeopardizing consumer safety and violating federal law.

Adulterated food A food shall be deemed to be adulterated- (a) Poisonous, insanitary, etc., ingredients (1) If it bears or contains any poisonous or deleterious substance which may render it injurious to health; but in case the substance is not an added substance such food shall not be considered adulterated under this clause if the quantity of such substance in such food does not ordinarily render it injurious to health. 1 (2)(A) if it

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the explicit guarantee that the products will be "free of contaminants" and "not be adulterated within the meaning of the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act." This omission contradicts the purpose of 21 U.S. Code § 342, which defines adulterated food and aims to prevent the distribution of products injurious to health. The original contract ensures compliance with this law, while the changed contract weakens this assurance, creating a potential loophole for non-compliant products. The scraped snippet 1 mentions "21 USC 342 : Adulterated food Text contains those laws in effect on June 14, 2025 From Title 21-FOOD AND DRUGS CHAPTER 9-FEDERAL FOOD, DRUG, AND COSMETIC ACT SUBCHAPTER IV-FOOD". This explicitly ties the omitted clause to a legal requirement, confirming that the omission creates a contradiction.
